sentences.text: --Brand.
Wonderful.
Hm?
Yeah.
The first time I talk so much.
K-R-Z-E-M-I-E-N-I-E-C.
Of course.
Russia Yeah.
I met my cousin, and I met his wife.
And I met four other Jewish people there, a doctor, his wife, and a boy-- and a son, and a couple without children, and her uncle, and my cousin, and me.
And she fed us all.
She was a business woman.
She saved his life.
This doctor was in the woods, somewhere.
The other people, I didn't know.
So she was trading American cigarettes for bread, for chickens, for-- for honey, for food.
And she fed us three meals a day, this woman.
Then she-- then he was drafted.
Russia organized a Polish army.
A Russian-Polish army.
And she drafted my cousin in the Russian-Polish army.
And she concealed her pregnancy and joined, volunteered in the Polish army, herself.
And the other Jewish people left, because they made their-- you know, they found a-- And I remained with the uncle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the uncle was a farmer.
And I went with him every Sunday to the church, to the Polish church, the Greek-- Greek Catholic church.
And yeah, and I joined-- I went to work in the hospital, a big hospital, 100 beds.
I was the only doctor.
There were 25 doctors in Krzemieniec before the war.
The Jews were killed, and the Polish doctors ran away with the-- with the-- with the Germans.
They'd rather be with the Germans than with the Russians.
They were afraid of Russians.
And I was in charge of the whole hospital and did operations without any much training.
We had all kind of diseases, you would not believe it, amputations and eye removals, pneumonias, and typhus fever.
We had a-- a-- a separate building, in the premise of the hospital, 40 beds for infectious diseases.
In town were 1,200 cases of infectious disease.
I got typhus fever.
I brought lice, every day, home.
By the way, I inherited this house with a maid.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I started to-- the maid was 80 years old.
She had a family to feed.
But she coming everyday from the other end of town to-- to-- to my house.
This was my house.
And I was working in the hospital every day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until I got sick and got typhus fever.
And there was a military medical unit, station there-- stationed temporarily.
They took me in a military ambulance to the hospital.
I was unconscious for three days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they lady, who-- I survive from chicken soup, Jewish chicken soup.
I didn't make it myself.
There was, next door, lived a-- a family, a doctor, a Jewish doctor, from Pidkamin, and his wife.
And she came, every day, to the hospital.
The hospital didn't have much food.
They didn't have nothing.
They had one pair of gloves, one nurse, a registered nurse.
The gloves were with holes, so the registered nurse darned the rubber gloves every day.
And she fed me chicken-- I didn't even know.
Through a-- through a straw, she fed me chicken soup.
To make the story short, I got-- I survived.
I went back home.
The only problem is it left my heart in a bad shape, a toxic myocarditis from this high fever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't work in the hospital.
My feet were swollen.
I walked-- I couldn't wear shoes.
I wore galoshes that I had made.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Incidentally, I worked in the hospital, and I worked in a clinic, have a outpatient, three hours.
Six hours in the hospital, three hours in the clinic.
And they paid me 902 rubles, all together.
That would be two pounds of butter a month. 600 pounds-- 600 rubles was a pound of butter.
And I got 900 rubles.
I couldn't make a living.
But the thing is, in the clinic, people came in with fake diseases and needed a certificate to get off work.
A shoemaker comes in.
He has to steal something from there, where he worked, because there was no private enterprise.
But it was a cooperative.
He steal.
There was a lot of American stuff.
American stuff, you know, shoe leather, canned foods, you have no idea, everything American.
Anybody, he says-- and I see he wants a-- a-- a fake certificate.
It's faked.
So I say, you come to see me in my home.
And I show him the holes in my shoes.
What are you, a shoemaker?
Well, give me a pair of.
So I-- I could give him up to 10 days off.
And so forth and so forth.
Anyway, when they-- the-- after typhus fever, I couldn't work.
And I-- I found a good-- a good, nice lady, a young-- not very young lady, who lost her husband.
And she took me to the hospital, a German hospital in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in Augsburg, in Augsburg, yeah.
I was already in Germany.
Because I had my-- I had my-- I-- I was in Germany, yeah, yeah, in Germany.
I came to Munich.
From Munich, I went to Augsburg.
And I developed this heart disease.
Before I-- before I-- before I-- oh, yeah!
I came-- I-- I came- I-- I told them I worked in this hospital.
And the secretary of the party, a woman, says I should join the-- the Communist Party.
I said, I'm too dumb, politically.
And I found out I have a sister in Cracow.
I have to look for my sister.
She let me go and brought a Mercedes-- a Mercedes Benz car to the house.
I should stay.
I say, no, I can't.
Anyway, I got a permit to leave.
And 11 days I traveled on-- on-- on top of a coal lorry to Cracow, to Poland.
In Poland, I found another cousin.
I delivered his baby.
His wife was pregnant.
She didn't want to go to the hospital.
I delivered this baby.
He gave me a gift, a leather-- to carry newspaper, papers.
And my son used this leather-- this-- this-- bag, leather bag through the high school.
He used it for when he was in school.
Anyway, I decided to go.
I want to get.
And-- and the Russians were there.
I don't want to go.
I don't stay with the Russian.
I want to get out from there.
I go to Prague.
I'm going to Prague.
Manage Austria first, then Prague.
And the Russians are there.
So I don't want to go to-- I don't want to stay with the Russians.
So I went to-- to Munich, to the American Zone.
That's where I landed.
And then I went to Augsburg, and I was working in the hospital.
But while I was in Germany, that's when my problem with my heart developed.
Because I had-- I had my-- my typhus fever in Russia.
But my heart problem, I developed in Germany.

And one lady took me to the-- to a famous hospital, a famous clinic, a famous-- I have all the medical-- medical reports, electrocardiogram and so forth, from this doctor, who was a professor in charge of-- of the internal medicine.
And then they treated me.
It was a homeopathic hospital, the-- a different little thing, little small portions.
Anyway, to make the story short, in Augsburg, I was working in a hospital a-- a year in luxurious conditions.
It was a-- it used to be a SS hospital.
But there was no future.
So I moved to Stuttgart.
The reason I moved to Stuttgart was because there was a American consulate and the Joint.
You know the Joint?
An American consulate.
And I went to the hospital in Stuttgart, again.
Because I was in bad shape.
And I couldn't walk.
In the meantime, I got in touch with my cousin in Chicago, to the HIAS.
You know HIAS?
He ran immigration, so forth.
I got a letter.
I was-- while I was in hospital, I got a letter.
[CRYING] I lived in a-- in a camp, you know, for-- for refugees, in a very beautiful camp.
They paid me with cigarettes.
I had a-- they sell-- they-- I was a doctor there.
I never saw patients.
But I was a doctor, so they gave me a private room.
And I got cigarettes.
And I got meals served in my room.
And then I went to the hospital.
And then I came from the hospital.
I start to make arrangement.
They did-- the-- the HIAS made arrangements.
I got a-- a ticket.
And I went to Bremerhaven and boarded a-- a ship to New York.
That's it.
New York, I came and put me in another shelter.
And I stayed in the shelter three days.
On the ship, I want-- I-- I heard about Coca Cola some miracles.
Coca Cola is-- is good for everything, for heart.
My heart got better.
And this and that, but it costs $0.50.
I had $15.
And I wanted Coca Cola in the worst way.
But I couldn't afford it, so I didn't buy it.
But when I came to New York, I walked around.
There's a square and a-- a-- a-- a vendor sells tangerines. 15 tangerines for $0.25.
I bought 15 tangerines, and I ate them up, all, everything.
And then I got a-- then I got a train ticket to Chicago.
My cousin picked me up.
And that's how I'm in Chicago.
And six months later, I met my wife, [SOBBING] and we got married.
No.
She came to-- 10 years before.
She came right after graduation, 1937.
When I graduate, she was already in-- she graduate in 1936.
When I came to-- 1940--
Huh?
And she was 35.
She was [SOBBING] five weeks younger.
[CRYING] 19th of March, she was born.
[CRYING] Died 29th of March.
[CRYING] Terrible.
[CRYING] Terrible.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] We had two beautiful children, right away.
So no time, we haven't got it.
[CRYING] She said, we haven't got the time.
So we have to have-- have children right away.
And so she-- my boy was born in-- in-- in '48, and my daughter was born in 1949. '9, no, 19-- she was born 194-- yeah, in 1949, '48, '49.
Yeah, that's right.
She practiced medicine.
We lived in Chicago.
I was train-- in training.
She had, already, a license and everything.
She had a housekeeper.
And she had a-- a apartment, a nice apartment.
She had a-- the-- the-- the-- the office and the apartment were together.
You know, she had four-- five bed-- five rooms.
So one-- one room was the dining room, one was her office, and the rest she has, too.
And Mayzie, Mayzie was the housekeeper.
She didn't let her open the refrigerator.
She did everything.
She did letter open--
Huh?
From-- from about, I would say, from where I come from 200-- 100-- 100 miles, 75, 80 miles, 100 mile.
It was from a small town, Nagorzanka.
The-- the-- the next big town is Stanislawow.
That used to be Austria.
When they asked her, where-- where were you born, what country were you born in, she said, Austria.
She was born, really, Austria, because till 1914-- till 1918, it was Austria.
It was-- Galicia was the district.
Because the western part but-- was belonged to Austria, Galicia.
And then Poland take it over in 1918.
But she never said.
She didn't want to say nothing about Poland.
She didn't care, not like me.
She didn't care for Poland.
I came to California, 1st of July, 1989.
First of all, my wife had asthma.
And we lived near Chicago.
In wintertime, she couldn't get out.
She could get out, but she had to wrap herself around.
We were riding in a car.
But take a walk, she had to cover herself.
It was very-- we had bad weather there, bad weather three, four months.
And I got tired of Calumet City except that Chicago was very close.
You take a-- a Dan Ryan train, 20 minutes, 30 minutes, you are in Chicago.
I went to Chicago every-- every Wednesday, we went to Chicago.
We would-- But Calumet City was a small town, going down.
At-- at one time, it was known for honky tonks, you know.
But there were small towns around a big-- a big temple was in Hammond, Indiana.
We lived on the border.
There is the Indiana-Illinois border.
Here is Illinois.
Here is Indiana.
And Calumet City was on the Illinois side.
And the-- and Hammond, a much bigger town, was on the Indiana side.
But then I decided to retire.
My wife was not feeling well.
And how long can you-- was-- I was, in-- in-- in '89, I was 77 years old. 77 years old, that's enough.
Some people are going to tell you more.
I had enough to live quietly.
We never spent too much money.
We didn't gamble.
We went to Florida.
We went to-- all over the world, all over.
Three times a year, I took off two weeks.
But I-- just, I decided to retire.
So-- and the climate, it was important.
And my son is in-- in Camarillo.
That's next to-- to Ventura, you know, next to Ventura.
And we have three grandchildren.
And-- and my daughter lived in-- and my daughter says, I'd like you to be in-- in Stuttgart, where I have three grandchildren.
[SOBBING] But Mommy has to be in California.
So that's it.
Hm?
Now?
Every Friday.
But it's not every Friday.
You see, I am-- I am-- they have their own life.
He's very busy.
He's a very good doctor.
And everybody likes him.
He has a tremendous practice.
She is a co-op.
She is administrator of the office.
She has three children.
One is retarded.
And it's in a home.
And she has beautiful two children at home, 12 year old Mitchell and 17-- 16 year old Rachel.
And she is a social.
She is beautiful.
She likes to dress.
She makes parties at home, 25 people.
We went there for the Thanksgiving party.
Well, it's a small party, 12 people, 14 people.
She's an excellent cook and baker.
And she is very active in the UJA, in the temple.
She has all kind of duties.
They appoint her-- Ellen Brand, Ellen Brand.
And she works in the office as their administrator.
Because nobody can-- she-- they have five employees.
But the-- the-- the most important things, she takes care.
Richard.
Jean.
In Stuttgart-- in Stuttgart?
In Rockford, Illinois.
Rockford, Illinois, that's 100 miles.
It's on the border with Wisconsin.
Of course.
Of course, she has-- she has three children, Abraham, Benjamin, and Shira.
Of course.
Of course.
They were bar mitzvah.
She didn't want bat mitzvah, because they-- there was not such a thing.
And they had Hebrew teachers and Hebrew school and whatever you-- whatever the temple had to offer, everything.
Grandchildren?
[LAUGHS] Grandchildren-- my Rachel teaches adults for-- prepares them for bar mitzvah.
There was an accountant, also from Brody.
From not Brody, close to Brody.
He lives next door to them.
He never was bar mitzvahed.
He has-- he is maybe 50-- no, more, 60-years-old.
He wants to be bar-- Rachel is teaching him.
Of course.
What are you talking?
Sure.
Of course.
Religious to a point, you know?
The-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was very religious.
We had all the holidays.
Everything was kosher.
And she had only Jewish friends, you know?
And she was-- she-- she subscribed, in-- in-- in Warsaw, there were two Yiddish papers, and
Oh, can-- oh, can I talk?
All right.
Of course.
Of course.
Sure.
I belonged to the temple.
I belonged to the Chabad.
And in Hammond, which is next door to Calumet City, I belonged to the temple, Temple Beth Israel.
My wife had a severe asthma.
Some people asthma-- some people have asthma, it's light, you know, nothing much.
You know, sometime you get short of breath.
What happened is when she-- when we were in Italy-- my wife has a sister in Italy.
And we came to Rome, fine.
We-- we want to go to Florence.
We went-- we planned to go to Rome, Florence, and-- and Venice.
And I said to my wife, you have to take this heavy coat, because the weather changed.
She said, I don't need a coat.
I'll take the light coat.
I said, you need the heavy coat.
She said, I don't need the heavy coat.
I need the light coat.
She caught a cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we came-- that's all.
To make the story short, when we came back home, to-- to Calumet City, we go shopping in Chicago, take a train.
And it's cold.
It's winter.
It's probably February or January.
We're walking in-- on State Street, which is the main street in Chicago.
Suddenly, she start to wheeze, wheeze, first time.
[PANTING BREATHS] I'm wheezing.
So we saw there was a apartment building-- not apartment building, a business building, with a big door, you know?
And you're walking into it.
And walking in this door, it was warm there.
And she got it over.
And that's how it started, asthma-- asthmatic.
She claims she had it when she was a child.
But I don't believe her, because we lived so many years together, and she never wheezed.
In California, she got worse.
It was a severe case of bronchial asthma.
And then she developed a-- a irregular heart.
We went-- she had seven doctors.
And she had all kind of different tests.
She had a pulmonologist, a cardiologist, and then another cardiologist, all kind of doctors.
And was-- she was taking cortisone.
The only thing which helps her was cortisone.
Cortisone damaged her heart.
Damaged her heart.
She had one-- one doctor-- she had irregular heart.
So one cardiologist said-- cardiologist said-- a good man-- he said, we don't treat that.
She has irregular heart, but that is-- she doesn't need no medicine.
But it wasn't getting better.
So I asked a-- a record.
There's a-- a Jewish doctor, from New York, and I met him, because he did some tests on me, when I came to visit with my son.
And he says, now we have to give her medicine.
She has digitalis-- digoxin and other things.
And they took EKGs and what.
Anyway, to make the story short, she didn't get better.
The asthma was getting worse.
And it-- and-- and go to the pulmonologist, the only thing I can offer, take 40 milligram.
Instead of 10 milligram, 40 milligram of digoxin-- of-- of-- of-- of a-- of cortisone.
And it didn't do.
She was up and down and up and down.
She was lie down.
She was tired.
She walked a lot.
She walked a lot.
She was not really sick a long time.
The last day, she slept in the-- in the morning.
I go to the gym.
And she says, I can't-- and she went to the gym.
No, to the park for a walk, Saturday.
I don't remember if it was Saturday.
But it was, anyway-- it was 29th of March.
And she says, I can't get out of bed.
I says, well, I thought you were ready already.
We want to go early.
Because we walked an hour.
[CRYING]
I go to her, Chupka, Chupka, [SOBBING] you're still in bed?
She didn't answer.
I look around.
She's dead.
She was dead.
I can't believe it.
She was dead.
That's all.
That's all.
When did--
I just called Ellen.
She called the rabbi right away.
[CRYING] I go to the cemetery twice a week.
I'm doing this stone now.
My next project is the stone, headstone.
It has to wait a year.
29Th of March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] That's all the story.
[CRYING] She loved this apartment.
You know, we worked together.
We traveled the world together, in Japan, Korea, Israel.
We were three times-- two times in Israel.
I was in Israel.
I just came back around a month ago.
And England, France-- she graduated in France.
She spoke French.
And we-- Italy, her sister is in Italy.
But her husband also dropped dead about three months-- six months ago.
Yeah.
I play the violin.
I can't play, because I don't see good.
OK.
All right.
That's it.
